text.title: Solo Niche Disqualifier Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules for filtering cold inbound to protect a freelancer's specialisation premium.

rule.statement: The freelancer maintains a written hard-no list — at least 5 named industries / company types / project shapes outside the specialisation. A lead whose brief matches any hard-no item is rejected without a call.
rule.rationale: Without a pre-written list, every lead gets the benefit of the doubt and lands in the discovery-call funnel, eroding capacity.

rule.statement: Each cold lead gets at most 5 minutes of evaluation time before deciding "discovery call" or "not a fit". 5 minutes is enough to scan a website, check LinkedIn, and read the inbound brief. Longer evaluations migrate decision quality into politeness and waste capacity.
rule.rationale: Solo operators systematically under-budget evaluation time and over-budget polite engagement. A hard cap converts a fuzzy "should I take this call" into a deterministic decision.

rule.statement: If the brief states or implies a budget below the floor rate, the lead is rejected. Acceptable evidence: "we have a $2k budget", "looking for entry-level", "should be quick — about 4 hours". No counter-offer at the floor, no negotiation.
rule.rationale: Counter-offers at the floor signal flexibility and invite further negotiation downward. A flat rejection preserves price discipline.

rule.statement: The brief must name the decision-maker (title or person). Briefs from "the team", "we're exploring options", or "I'll need to check with my co-founder before deciding anything" are rejected as not-yet-ready.
rule.rationale: Multi-stakeholder briefs without a named decision-maker convert at <5% and consume 3x more discovery time. Filtering them out at intake protects pipeline health.

rule.statement: When a lead is rejected on a disqualifier signal, the reply is a single short paragraph that does NOT propose alternative pricing, alternative scope, or a future-fit window. Forbidden phrases: "but if you bump to $X", "happy to do a smaller scope", "circle back next quarter".
rule.rationale: Counter-offers in rejection emails reopen negotiation and re-introduce the leak the filter exists to close.

example.code:
Hi Sam,
Thanks for the note. Crypto exchanges sit outside the work I take on, so this isn't going to be a fit. I'd point you toward the r/freelancedev hire board for folks who specialise there.
Best, A
example.note: Names the disqualifier (industry mismatch), no counter-offer, gives a low-friction alternative, under 60 words.

antipattern.description: Lead writes "I have a $1k budget for a 4-week project" — freelancer replies "could you stretch to $3k? I might be able to make it work" and ends up running a $3k version of a $12k project.
antipattern.reason: Violates r3 and r5. Counter-offers at the disqualifier signal anchor the freelancer to a discount, training the inbound channel to under-bid.
